title: Paths, Navigation, and Filesystem Hierarchy · System Lens: Hierarchy Purpose
summary: Locate application configuration and logs on a workstation whose current directory and distribution conventions differ from a familiar machine. Planned artifact: Add and verify the linux hierarchy purpose increment in a path-resolution and filesystem-map notebook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Relate root-level directories to boot, configuration, devices, variable data, user data, runtime state, and optional software roles.
Use pwd, cd, and directory stacks while predicting the resulting working directory before each transition.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: On a worked example host, learner starts in /home/learner/lab with Bash 5.3, Coreutils 9.11, and exit status 1. Resolve ./data/../scripts, identify command authority, and separate terminal, shell, process, and kernel evidence. Competency probe: Relate root-level directories to boot, configuration, devices, variable data, user data, runtime state, and optional software roles. A defensible solution applies Hierarchy Purpose, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a path-resolution and filesystem-map notebook.

content.code:
# Worked focus: linux-hierarchy-purpose — Relate root-level directories to boot, configuration, devices, variable data, user data, runtime state, and optional software roles.
pwd
printf 'shell=%s\n' "$BASH_VERSION"
type -a printf
command -V pwd
printf 'status=%d\n' "$?"
content.caption: Runnable safe terminal evidence example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.
